narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've put into your law school dreams, and I want to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we'd hoped, landing at a 41% chance of not passing. Picture your academic journey as the opening chapters of your story: your Year 1 grades placed you in the bottom decile at school, which was the biggest signal to the model that early struggles might carry forward, pulling strongly toward the risk of failure. By Year 3, that position had improved only slightly to the second decile, reinforcing those concerns as the second-most influential factor without showing the kind of upward momentum the model associates with bar success. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 31, which, while respectable, didn't stand out enough in this competitive field to counterbalance those trends, marking it as another key push toward caution. Your lower-middle family income added to the picture, as the model sees resource constraints like this often making the intense bar prep grind tougher without extra support. Your Asian background and male gender fit patterns the model links to higher hurdles in this arena, subtly tipping the scales further. Even your solid 2.9 undergraduate GPA, while helpful, couldn't fully offset these elements in the ranking. The one bright spot was your choice to go full-time, which nudged things positively toward passing by letting you focus fully on studies. Together, these pieces painted a portrait of someone with real potential but facing an uphill battle against early academic dips and external pressures that have tripped up others before. It's not a definitive "no" on your abilities—many have rewritten similar stories through targeted prep—but the model flagged this combination as too uncertain for admission right now. What stands out most is how much those Year 1 and 3 deciles shaped the narrative; imagine channeling extra effort there in future pursuits. Hang in there; this is just one page, and your next chapter could shift everything.
